content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 2, 2012

William R. Smith
7594 California City Blvd.
California City, CA 93505

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-12-096

Dear Mr. Smith:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ The Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(3)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

Please also note that there are other bodies of law, separate and apart from the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions that may apply to your situation. Our advice is based solely on the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application of other incompatible activities and conflict-of-interests laws that may apply including, but not limited to, common law conflict of interest and Government Code Section 1090. We strongly suggest that you consult with the city attorney for advice relating to whether or not Government Code Section 1090 may be implicated by your situation.

QUESTION

Do you have a conflict of interest that would prevent you from participating in decisions related to Ace-City Hardware?

CONCLUSION

Since you have an economic interest in Ace-City Hardware and the purchasers of the hardware store as a source of income, you may not participate in decisions regarding Ace-City Hardware until 12 months after the date you received the final payment for the sale of the hardware store.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-12-096
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a member of the California City City Council. You owned Ace-City Hardware
from 1993 until January of 2012, at which point you sold it to your sons. You continue to own
the property on which the store is located. The city has made purchases from the store since it
opened in 1993, and will likely continue doing so in the future. You state that the city would
suffer financial hardship if it could not purchase from Ace-City Hardware because of the
additional expenses associated with travelling to the nearest alternative, which is located in
another city 25-30 miles away.

Upon your election to city council in 2008, you asked the City Manager whether your
ownership of Ace-City Hardware created a conflict of interest, and received advice from the City
Attorney via the City Manager that no conflict of interest would arise so long as you abstained
from voting or influencing a governmental decision related to your store. On May 22, 2012, you
were advised that you may have a conflict of interest. You now seek advice on whether you
have a conflict of interest.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, no public official shall make, participate in making, or in any way
attempt to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which he or she
knows or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest.

The Commission has developed an eight-step process for deciding whether an official has
a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision; we generally describe this
process below. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Steps One and Two: Are you a "public official" making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?

As a member of the city council, you are a public official under the Act. (Section 82048.)
As a city council member, you will be called upon to make decisions related to contracting with
Ace-City Hardware for the purchase of goods, approving purchases, paying for invoices, and
other related issues.² Therefore, you will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using
your official position to influence a governmental decision.³

¹ A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or
her office, votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or
enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18701.1.) Additionally, a public
official "participates in making a governmental decision" when he or she is involved in negotiations without
significant substantive review or provides advice or makes recommendations. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public
official will also be "influencing" a governmental decision when he or she contacts, appears before, or otherwise
attempts to influence any member, officer, employee, or consultant of the agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

³ Please note, if a public official has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he
or she must: (1) publicly identify the financial interest immediately prior to discussion of the item, as detailed in

File No. A-12-096
Page No. 3

Step Three: Do you have a potentially disqualifying economic interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• An economic interest in a source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• An economic interest in a source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)¹

Source of Income: A public official has an economic interest in any person or business from whom he or she has received income of $500 or more within the 12 months preceding the relevant governmental decision. (Regulation 18703.3.) "Income" is a payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the filer, or reimbursement for expenses. (Section 82030(a).)

You state that you sold Ace-City Hardware in January of 2012. We assume that you were receiving more than $500 in income from ownership of Ace-City Hardware up until the

¹ Regulation 18702.5(b): (2) recuse himself or herself from discussing, voting on, or otherwise influencing the matter; and (3) leave the room until after the discussion, vote, and conclusion of any other disposition of the matter.(Section 87105.) Special rules for closed sessions, calendars, absences, and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests are discussed in Regulation 18702.5(c) and (d).

⁴ "Immediate family" includes one's spouse and dependent children. (Section 82029.) "Dependent child" is defined as a child of a public official who is under 18 years old and whom the official is entitled to claim as a dependent on his or her federal tax return. (Regulation 18229.1.) If either of your sons meets this definition, then you will have an economic interest under the personal financial effects rule.

File No. A-12-096
Page No. 4

time of the sale. We also assume you received more than $500 in the sale of the hardware store to your sons. You therefore have an economic interest in Ace-City Hardware and in your sons, the purchasers, as sources of income for the 12 months following the final payment from these sources for the sale of the hardware store.²

You also have an interest in real property, but that interest does not appear to be implicated in your facts. We do not further analyze this economic interest.

Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision?

Regulation 18704(a) provides:

"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision. If a public official's economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is indirectly involved."

A source of income is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when the source initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made, or is a named party or the subject of the proceeding concerning the decision. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a governmental decision involves any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the source of income. (See Regulation 18704.1.)

You state that California City makes purchases from Ace-City Hardware. When the city council makes decisions involving purchases from Ace-City hardware, the store and your sons, as purchasers, are considered the subject of the proceeding, and your economic interest is directly involved in the governmental decision.

Steps Five and Six: Are the financial impacts on your economic interests "material" and "reasonably foreseeable"?

A conflict of interest arises only when the financial impacts of a public official's economic interest are both reasonably foreseeable and material. (Regulation 18700(a).) Regulation 18706(b) provides a non-exclusive list of general guidelines for determining whether an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, which includes:

(1) The extent to which the official of-the official's source of income has engaged, is engaged, or plans on engaging in business activity in the jurisdiction;

² If you receive $500 or more per year from Ace-City Hardware's current owners through a lease agreement or otherwise, then you have an additional economic interest in a source of income for 12 months after each payment aggregating $500 or more. You have not provided any facts about the lease arrangement, therefore we will not provide further analysis regarding this potential economic interest.

File No. A-12-096
Page No. 5


(2) The market share held by the official or the official's source of income in the
jurisdiction;

(3) The extent to which the official or the official's source of income has competition for
business in the jurisdiction;

(4) The scope of the governmental decision in question; and

(5) The extent to which the occurrence of the material financial effect is contingent upon
intervening events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's agency,
or any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's
agency.

Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably foreseeable at the
time the decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. An economic effect
need not be certain to be reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person, or business, who is a source of
income to a public official and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency,
is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)

Since you state that the city has a long history of purchasing goods from Ace-City
Hardware and that it creates a financial hardship on California City to purchase from an
alternative hardware store, it is very likely that the city will continue to make purchases from the
hardware store, which will result in a subsequent profit for the business. Therefore, the financial
impact of the governmental decision on Ace-City Hardware and your sons is both reasonably
foreseeable and material.

Steps Seven and Eight: If you have a conflict of interest, does either the "public
generally" exception or the "legally required participation" exception apply?

Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)), or if the official is
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708).

You have not presented any facts indicating that either exception is applicable to your
circumstances, so we will not address them further.

File No. A-12-096
Page No. 6


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Lindsey Nakano
Legal Intern

LN:jgl
